coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence “turn-ON” nanosensor for cyanide ion using supramolecular hybrid of graphene quantum dots and cobalt pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanine
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: A functional hybrid of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cobalt pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanine (CoPc) used as a sensitive nanoprobe for the recognition of cyanide ion (CN−) is described in this work. The fluorescence of GQDs was quenched upon non-covalent (π-π stacking) hybrid formation with CoPc via a possible energy transfer pathway. However, in the presence of CN−, the interaction between GQDs and CoPc was perturbed, such that the fluorescence of GQDs initially quenched by CoPc was found to be efficiently recovered in the presence of CN−. Amongst the molecules and anions tested to ascertain their effects on the fluorescence behaviour of the hybrid, only CN− ion induced the tunable “ off-on ” restoration of the fluorescence of GQDs, which demonstrates the selectivity of the hybrid towards CN−. The restored fluorescence signals of the GQDs were linearly modulated by different concentrations of CN− and were used for the quantitative assay of CN− with high sensitivity coupled with rapid detection time. The detection was in the linear range of 1.0–50.0 nM with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.5 nM. The analysis of spiked samples for the recovery of CN− further demonstrated the applicability of the hybrid for the satisfactory detection of the target analyte.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cobalt pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanine
Fluorescence “turn ON”
Cyanide ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for the fluorescence sensing of cyanide ion (CN−)
Recovery studies (analytical applicability of the probes)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology and National Research Foundation , South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of as-synthesized GQDs (A) wide scan survey and (B) C1s high resolution.
(A) UV/Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQDs and (B) UV–Vis absorption of GQDs-CoPc hybrid and CoPc. Solvent – PBS (10 mM, pH 7.2)/DMSO (3:1).
FTIR spectra of GQDs, GQDs-CoPc hybrid and CoPc alone.
TEM images of GQDs (A) and GQDs-CoPc hybrid (B).
Dynamic light scattering experiment showing the size of (A) GQDs and (B) GQDs-CoPc hybrid.
Spectra showing fluorescence quenching effect of CoPc on GQDs upon conjugation. Solvent – PBS (10 mM, pH 7.2)/DMSO (3:1).

(A) Fluorescence “turn ON” signal of GQDs-CoPc in the presence of different concentration of CN−. (B) Linear portion of the calibration plot for CN− detection. Solvent – PBS (10 mM, pH 7.2)/DMSO (3:1).
UV–Vis absorption spectral changes of CoPc in the presence of different amounts of CN− (50 and 10 nM) showing the possible axial ligation of CN− to CoPc. Solvent – PBS (10 mM, pH 7.2)/DMSO (3:1).
Fluorescence spectra of GQDs alone recorded in the presence of different concentration of CN− showing no effect on GQDs. Solvent - PBS (10 mM, pH 7.2)/DMSO (3:1).

The results of selectivity studies involving anions with potential to interfere in the quantitative detection of CN−. All interfering species concentration = 20 μM (except for F−, Cl− and Br− which = 5 μM).
A depiction of the synthesis pathway of GQDs-CoPc supramolecular hybrid and the fluorescence quenching of GQDs.
Graphical illustration of the mechanism for CN− sensing by supramolecular hybrid of GQDs-CoPc. The axial ligation of CN− to CoPc is shown.
The comparison of reported LODs for some CN− probes with the developed probe in this work.
The recovery/detection of CN− in spiked water samples collected from tap using the proposed probe (GQDs-CoPc).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence “turn-ON” nanosensor for cyanide ion using supramolecular hybrid of graphene quantum dots and cobalt pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A functional hybrid of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cobalt pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanine (CoPc) used as a sensitive nanoprobe for the recognition of cyanide ion (CN−) is described in this work. The fluorescence of GQDs was quenched upon non-covalent (π-π stacking) hybrid formation with CoPc via a possible energy transfer pathway. However, in the presence of CN−, the interaction between GQDs and CoPc was perturbed, such that the fluorescence of GQDs initially quenched by CoPc was found to be efficiently recovered in the presence of CN−. Amongst the molecules and anions tested to ascertain their effects on the fluorescence behaviour of the hybrid, only CN− ion induced the tunable “ off-on ” restoration of the fluorescence of GQDs, which demonstrates the selectivity of the hybrid towards CN−. The restored fluorescence signals of the GQDs were linearly modulated by different concentrations of CN− and were used for the quantitative assay of CN− with high sensitivity coupled with rapid detection time. The detection was in the linear range of 1.0–50.0 nM with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.5 nM. The analysis of spiked samples for the recovery of CN− further demonstrated the applicability of the hybrid for the satisfactory detection of the target analyte.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the delecterious effects of cyanide ions (CN−) to biological and environmental processes, the sensitive and selective detection of this extremely toxic anion is highly imperative. Analytical methods devised over the years for the recognition of CN− have included chromatographic [1], electrochemical [2], fluorimetric [3], electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometric [4] and spectrophotometric processes [5–7]. Interestingly, amongst the aforementioned techniques, fluorimetric techniques are most sensitive, simple and cost effective [8,9]. The fluorimetric probes reported to date for CN− detection are based on fluorescence “ off-on ”, “ on-off ” and/or ratiometric processes which have shown to be highly sensitive [3,8–10]. For instance, boronic acid derivatives [11], CdSe/CdTe quantum dots (QDs) [12,13], ruthenium (II)-pyridine and rhenium-polypyridyl complexes [14,15], Zn(II) prophyrin/crown ether conjugates [16], zincon-Cu2+ complex [17], coumarin Shiff's base [18] and boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY)-borane adducts [19] have been reportedly employed as CN− sensors. However, many of the deployed probe materials may exhibit some level of toxicity with interferences from other anions such F−, Cl−, Br− and SCN− thereby requiring lenthy pre-treatment steps before quantitative analysis [8,9]. Hence, there is a need to deploy environmentally friendly and biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials as sensors that can ensure the selective detection of CN−. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their derivatives are promising in this regard.

GQDs are interesting carbon-based nanomaterials due to their high and tunable photoluminescence (PL), ease of functionalization/conjugation with other molecules (due to π electron system and surface functional groups), non-toxicity and biocompatibility [20–22]. This has motivated an increase in their utilization in nanosensors design, bioimaging and drug delivery [23–25]. The coordination of graphene and graphitic carbon nitride QDs to macrocyclic complexes of phthalocyanines (Pcs) and porphyrins (Ps) have been reported and deployed in sensing, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [26–31]. We have shown in our earlier studies, the potential of nanoconjugates of GQDs and Pcs to act as excellent fluorescence nanoprobes for the detection of analytes such as biothiols, Hg2+ and ascorbic acid [27,28,32]. In our previous studies, fluorescence behaviour of GQDs in the presence of Zn and Co pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanines and porphyrins were elucidated, where the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched by the respective complexes [33]. The quenching was however not used for sensing. Hence, this work further explores the sensing ability of the hybrid of GQDs and CoPc (ZnPc complexes showed no effect of tested analytes) whereby different molecules (such as anions, cations and amino acids) were tested in order to induce “ off-on ” fluorescence behaviour of GQDs (within the hybrid). Results indicated that only CN− could induce a “ turn ON ” of the fluorescence of GQDs leading to the derivation of a very specific and selective nanoprobe for the detection of CN−. The detection mechanism was elucidated to be based on the ability of CN− to coordinate with the Co metal of the Pc complex via axial ligation, thereby perturbing the non-covalent (π-π stacking) interaction between GQDs and CoPc, with a consequent modulated recovery of the fluorescence of the latter. The results obtained in this study demonstrated the ability of the supramolecular hybrid to sensitively and selectively detect CN− within nanomolar (nM) concentration without any interference from other selected anions.

Graphene oxide and dialysis membrane tubing (MWCO 1.5 kDa) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium cyanide (CAUTION) was obtained from Merck. Absolute ethanol, methanol and hexane were purchased from SAARCHEM. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and all other aqueous solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water obtained from a Mili-Q Water system (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). Cobalt phthalocyanine 1-pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde (CoPc) was synthesized according to procedures previously reported [33].
Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80 kV.

X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer. A hybrid lens was used to acquire the high resolution scans at 160 eV pass energy in slot mode. The centre used for the scans was at 520 eV (width of 1205 eV) with steps at 1 eV and dwell time at 100 ms. The core level binding energies (BEs) were aligned with respect to the adventitious C1s binding energy (BE) of 284 eV. A nonlinear least squares procedure was employed for curve fitting using Origin Pro application.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90. Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Ground state electronic absorption was performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range of 300–800 nm. Infrared spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum-ATR. A Metrohm Swiss 827 pH meter was used for pH measurements. Hydrothermal synthesis was carried out using a Berghof (Germany) High Pressure Laboratory Reactor (highpreactor) BR-300, V.3.0 equipped with PT-100 temperature and pressure sensors, BTC-300 Temperature regulator and manometer with PTFE lining.

GQDs were synthesized by a top-down hydrothermal process using a method reported previously [34]. Briefly, GO (0.5 g) was oxidized in concentrated H2SO4 (10 mL) and HNO3 (30 mL) for 4 h under ultrasonication. The mixture was then diluted with Millipore water (100 mL) and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The pH of the oxidized GO was adjusted to 8.0 using 10% NaOH. The obtained suspension was transferred to a 400 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated up to 200 °C for 12 h. The product was left to cool to room temperature and filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. The collected colloidal solution was further dialyzed for two days using a dialysis membrane (MW 1.5 kDa) to remove excess acids and salt. Powdered GQDs were obtained by freeze drying the colloidal solution.

The preparation of the hybrid of GQDs-CoPc was carried out by adding GQDs (2 mg) to CoPc (0.2 mg) in dry DMSO (5 mL). The resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 2 h, followed by stirring in the dark for 48 h. A mixture of water, ethanol, methanol and hexane (5:3:2) was used to repeatedly wash the product so as to dispel the excess uncoordinated GQDs and CoPc by centrifuging until the solution in the centrifuge tubes turned colourless. The solid product was dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for the fluorescence sensing of cyanide ion (CN−)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, GQDs-CoPc (2 mg) was dissolved in DMSO/PBS (10 mM, pH 7.2, 1:3) followed by brief ultrasonication to ensure proper dispersion of the solute. Aliquots of the probe solution (2 mL) were introduced into different vials. This was followed by sequential titration with aqueous solutions of CN− at different concentrations (1.0–200 nM) at fixed intervals. The resulting mixtures were transferred in turn into a quartz cell and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after allowing an incubation time of 1 min at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

The solvent employed was a mixture of DMSO and PBS (pH 7.2). DMSO was employed to in order to allow for the complete solubility of CoPc. The effect of pH within the acidic range was not investigated since in acidic condition the detection of CN− could lead to the formation of the toxic hydrogen cyanide (HCN) [35]. Also, the fluorescence emissions of GQDs are known to be at optimum between pH 7-8 [36], hence PBS solution of pH 7.2 was employed. Analysis of spiked samples and selectivity experiments were performed under similar experimental procedures and conditions.
The as-synthesized GQDs were characterized prior to being conjugated to CoPc.

X-ray photoemission (XPS) chemical analysis of the GQDs showed the presence of C and O as the prominent elements at 284 and 530 eV, respectively (Fig. 1 A) in wide scan. The core level high-resolution carbon species (C1s) were deconvoluted into three chemically distinct components centered at 284.7, 286.3, and 288.2 eV, Fig. 1B, which are attributed to sp2 C (CC), sp3 C (CC) and oxidized C (CO, CO), respectively.
Scheme 1 shows a representation of the supramolecular hybrid of GQDs-CoPc formed via π–π stacking.

Fig. 2B compares the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CoPc alone and GQDs-CoPc hybrid. Interaction between GQDs and CoPc results in the red-shift of the Q band of the latter from 706 for CoPc to 716 nm upon conjugation to GQDs (Table S1). Similar red-shifts have been reported in non-covalent (π-π) composites of Pcs with other carbon-based materials such as GO [37]. In addition, the spectrum of the hybrid conjugate in region below 600 nm (Fig. 2B) was greatly modified due to the presence of GQDs showing the hybrid formation.

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum for GQDs-CoPc showed the broad band absorption which corresponds to the presence of OH groups of the GQDs at 3417 cm−1 (Fig. 3 ) which is similarly present in the GQDs alone but at ∼3378 cm−1. The characteristic CO and CC-stretching vibrations (1632 and 1411 cm−1) of GQDs alone also appeared in the hybrid at about 1640 and 1391 cm−1, respectively, overlapping the CC and CC vibrations of CoPc alone at 1631 and 1377 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 3).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to determine the morphology and size distribution of GQDs and their CoPc hybrid as shown in Fig. 4 . GQDs-CoPc formed a cluster-like hybrid due to aggregation of the materials (Fig. 4B) as compared to the GQDs alone with monodispersed particles (Fig. 4A). Aggregation resulting from π-π stacking of Pcs onto nanomaterials have been commonly described [38,39].

Further, the change in size resulting from the hybrid formation was confirmed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment. The hydrodynamic size for GQDs-CoPc hybrid (∼30 nm) increased relative to GQDs alone (Av. size of 8 nm), Fig. 5 . This result points to the formation of a nanohybrid of GQDs and CoPc.

The fluorescence behaviour of GQDs upon hybrid formation with CoPc complex was investigated. It was observed that the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched upon conjugation to CoPc (Fig. 6 ). Hence, GQDs-CoPc hybrid exhibited a “donor-acceptor” relationship with a mechanistic pathway involving the transfer of energy from GQDs to CoPc complex. Similar observations have been previously reported in the literature where the fluorescence of GQDs is quenched upon coordination to phthalocyanines and porphyrins [29,30,34].

In order to evaluate the fluorescence “turn ON” sensitivity of the GQDs-CoPc probe, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-CoPc in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of CN− were evaluated using Eq. (1) [40].
(1)
ΔF
F 0
= 1 + K
[ C
N −
]
where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of CN−, respectively. K is the fluorescence enhancement constant and [CN−] is the concentration of CN−. Note that ΔF/F0 was used due to fluorescence enhancement instead of F0/F normally used to indicate a fluorescence quenching process. K values were used to assess the sensitivity of the nanoprobe. The fluorescence signals increased linearly with the concentrations of CN− within the linear concentration range of 1–50 nM, Fig. 7B (See supporting information for full calibration plot, Fig. S1). The limit of detection (LOD) were evaluated from the plot shown in Fig. 7B and using Eq. (2).
(2)
L O D = 3 δ / K
where δ is the standard deviation of blank measurement (n = 12) and K is the slope of the calibration graph, Fig. 7B. The LOD obtained for CN− detection was 0.5 nM. It follows from the obtained LOD that GQDs-CoPc assay for CN− is highly sensitive as compared to other nanoprobes reported to date, some of which are shown in Table 1 .

The sensing mechanism of the designed probe (GQDs-CoPc hybrid) could be explained on the basis of the interaction between CoPc (within the hybrid) and CN− via axial ligation (metal-ligand coordination), thus increasing the centre-centre distance between GQDs and CoPc as depicted in Scheme 2.

This ligation could possibly lead to the disruption of the “donor-acceptor” relationship of GQDs and CoPc resulting in the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (Fig. 7A) which was quenched initially by the CoPc (Fig. 6). Axial ligation of CN− to CoPc was proved using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The studies for axial ligation of CN− were done for the uncoordinated CoPc and the observed changes in the Q-band of CoPc in the presence of CN (at different concentrations) were typical of axial ligation, as the coordination of cyanide to CoPc complexes is known to result in dicyano complexes [49–51]. The Q-band of the CoPc red-shifted [2] as different concentrations of CN were added to CoPc solution, Fig. 8 , further suggesting axial ligation of the CoPc as depicted in Scheme 2. Hence, we propose in this work that CN− may have changed the orientation of the CoPc within the GQDs-CoPc hybrid via axial ligation resulting in the enhancement of the fluorescence of GQDs.

Further, it was observed that the fluorescence of GQDs was not affected by CN− since the fluorescence of GQDs (alone) remained relatively unchanged (Fig. 9 ) at different concentrations of CN−. This observation was a demonstration of the fact that the “turn ON” fluorescence signals of GQDs within the hybrid were as a result of the effect of the axial ligation of CN− to the CoPc.

The selectivity of GQDs-CoPc probe was tested by subjecting a mixture of common anions that have been implicated to often interfere in CN− detection in water samples. Anions such as F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, H2PO4 −, HSO4 −, NO3 −, ClO4 −, SCN−, AcO−, HSO3 −, SO4 2− and CO3 2− were tested in order to evaluate the selective detection of CN−. As shown in Fig. 10 , the difference in the recovered fluorescence of GQDs in the presence of CN− relative to the other anions is a demonstration of the good selectivity for CN− recognition by the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery studies (analytical applicability of the probes)
ce:section.ce:para: Following the devised procedures, real water samples collected from the tap was analyzed. Table 2 shows the results obtained as the samples were spiked with known concentrations of CN− (0.5–50 nM) and detection was carried out via the standard addition method. The percentage recoveries were between 96.8-101%, with a relative standard deviation of <5 (for replicate measurements, n = 5). The obtained result is a further demonstration of the high capability of the designed probe system to analyze trace levels of CN− ion in a real sample.

This work demonstrated a rapid and sensitive fluorescence “switch ON” assay for cyanide ion (CN−) based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc) hybrid system. The ability of CN− to axially coordinate to CoPc strongly leads to the hindering of the “donor-acceptor” relationship between GQDs and CoPc, whereby the fluorescence of GQDs initially quenched by CoPc is restored due to the inhibition of the quenching effect of CoPc on GQDs in the presence CN−. The restored fluorescence of GQDs was modulated by varying concentrations of CN−, thus affording the quantitative detection of CN− as a target analyte. This sensing system showed to be advantageous with respect to the detection of CN− in trace level (nanomolar range sensitivity) with high selectivity. Analytical applicability of the system was further demonstrated by analysis of tap water samples spiked with known amounts of CN−.
The authors declare no potential conflicts of interest with regard to this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology and National Research Foundation, South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.08.038.
